summary: Graham argues that tech companies are right to push for easier immigration because exceptional programmers are rare and cannot be produced just through training. Since the US has only about 5% of the world’s population, most great programmers are born elsewhere, and letting them in is crucial to maintaining US technological leadership. He warns that failing to do so could shift the global talent hub away from the US with long-term consequences.
key_points: Exceptional programmers are orders of magnitude more valuable and cannot be produced by training alone.
Given population realities, most great programmers are born outside the US.
Tech companies seek immigration reform because of genuine talent scarcity, not wage suppression.
Concentrations of top talent create powerful hubs, so attracting them is strategically vital.
money_quotes: We have the potential to ensure that the US remains a technology superpower just by letting in a few thousand great programmers a year.
It could easily be the defining mistake this generation of American politicians later become famous for.
So please, get on with it.
key_insight: Because exceptional programmers are rare and globally distributed, immigration is the practical lever that determines whether the US remains a top technology hub.
